name: SQL Injection
description: A malicious user inserts SQL code into an input field to manipulate the database.
example: The attacker enters: ' OR '1'='1 into a login form, bypassing authentication entirely without a valid password.
defence: Use PDO prepared statements — never concatenate user input into queries. Validate and sanitise all inputs server-side before processing.

name: XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)
description: Attacker injects malicious scripts into pages viewed by other users, stealing sessions or redirecting victims.
example: A comment field accepts: [script tag] alert(document.cookie) [/script] — when viewed by other users, their session tokens are exposed.
defence: Always escape output with htmlspecialchars(). Set Content-Security-Policy headers. Never use innerHTML with user-supplied content.

name: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery)
description: Tricks authenticated users into submitting unintended requests — such as changing passwords or transferring funds — from another site.
example: A hidden form on evil.com auto-submits a bank transfer while the victim is logged into their bank in another tab.
defence: Generate a unique CSRF token per session. Validate on every state-changing POST request. Use SameSite=Strict cookies.

name: Brute Force & Rate Limiting
description: Automated tools attempt thousands of password combinations per minute to compromise user accounts or abuse API endpoints.
example: An attacker sends 50,000 login attempts per hour against /login using a list of common passwords until one succeeds.
defence: Track failed attempts by IP in the database. Lock accounts after N failures. Enforce CAPTCHA after 3 attempts. Require strong passwords.
code_lines: // Count recent attempts from this IP:
die('Too many attempts. Try again later.');

name: Insecure File Upload
description: Attacker uploads a malicious script disguised as an image or document, which can then be executed on the server.
example: A file named shell.php is uploaded through an avatar upload form. If executed, it gives the attacker full server command access.
defence: Validate MIME type server-side (not just extension). Rename all uploaded files randomly. Store uploads outside the web root. Never execute uploaded files.

name: Sensitive Data Exposure
description: Credentials, API keys, or personal data are stored or transmitted insecurely — in plain text, logs, or public repositories.
example: A developer commits a config file containing database passwords to a public GitHub repo. Bots scan and exploit it within minutes.
defence: Hash passwords with password_hash(). Store secrets in .env files (excluded from git). Encrypt sensitive DB fields. Use HTTPS everywhere without exception.

name: Session Hijacking
description: Attacker steals or predicts a valid session token to impersonate an authenticated user without knowing their password.
example: A session ID appears in the URL: example.com/dashboard?PHPSESSID=abc123. It gets captured from browser history or a shared link.
defence: Use HttpOnly and Secure cookie flags. Regenerate session ID immediately after login. Set short session timeouts. Never expose session IDs in URLs.

name: Directory Traversal
description: Attacker manipulates file path inputs using ../ sequences to access files outside the intended directory, including system files.
example: Request to /download.php?file=../../config/database.php exposes database credentials stored outside the web root.
defence: Validate file paths with realpath(). Use basename() to strip directory components. Confirm the resolved path starts with your intended base directory.
code_lines: // Confirm it stays within the base directory:
